statusLine: European fuel stocks remain under pressure with 19 of 27 countries holding critical diesel reserves and jet fuel shortages affecting 17 nations as Brent crude reaches $93.51 per barrel.
fullAnalysis:
European fuel security faces significant strain as emergency stock releases to counter Middle East supply disruptions have left most EU countries below safe reserve levels. Despite EU averages of 96.3 days for petrol and 88.8 days for diesel appearing adequate, the reality shows deep disparities across member states. Diesel stocks present the most acute concern, with 19 of 27 countries holding critical reserves well below the 90-day mandatory minimum, including major economies like Germany (95.6 days) barely above threshold and France (89.3 days) in watch status.

Jet fuel shortages compound the crisis, affecting 17 countries at critical levels. Poland faces particularly severe shortages across all fuel types, with jet fuel reserves at just 22.5 days. Eastern European nations including Romania, Austria, and the Baltic states show mixed patterns, with some maintaining strong diesel reserves while struggling with petrol and jet fuel availability.

The 29% surge in Brent crude prices since late February—from $72.48 to $93.51 per barrel—reflects ongoing supply tensions and is translating into higher pump prices across Europe. Danish consumers face the highest petrol costs at €2.392 per liter, while Malta offers the cheapest at €1.34. This price disparity, combined with uneven stock distributions, highlights the fragmented nature of Europe's current fuel security challenge.
keyPoints: 19 of 27 EU countries hold critical diesel reserves below safe levels, with major economies like France at watch status
Jet fuel shortages affect 17 countries critically, with Poland's reserves at just 22.5 days
Brent crude has surged 29% since late February to $93.51/barrel, driving fuel price increases across Europe
Eastern Europe faces acute shortages, while Nordic countries generally maintain stronger reserve positions
Pump price disparities are extreme, with Danish petrol at €2.392/L compared to Malta's €1.34/L
